item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rather than accidental cloning of hazmat suit, wouldn't the tree already fill the room with cloning of microscopic objects in contact with the tree all the time as there is no statement about the lower limit of the mass of object being cloned.

A weight limit of 90.9 kg (200 lb) per object has been previously recorded.

Should the original author"s weight limit change to mass limit as kilogram(kg)/pound(lb) is used instead of Newton(N).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article mentions TWO other SCPs. only TWO.

One of the crosslinks is SCP-173, which in-universe doesn't really have any meaningful justification for its repeated crosstesting. IRL, it's a very popular article, so of course people like to namedrop/link it. For some readers, gratuitous mentioning of popular articles would be considered excessive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get what you guys are talking about when you accuse it of excessive cross-testing. This article mentions TWO other SCPs. only TWO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An interesting idea would be that anything edible it cloned would taste like apples, being as it is for all intents and purposes barring it's unique properties an apple tree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the foundation would prefer throwing away additional hazmat suits rather than disposing of human beings with the lifespan of a fruit.

item.title: Mod Post
item.content:encoded:
Doesn't matter.

Apparently this has been explained to you multiple times. Stop. The answer isn't changing and all you will accomplish is annoying us.

item.title: Re: Staff Post- Rewrite Captain
item.content:encoded: but the original authors account was deleted

item.title: Staff Post- Rewrite Captain
item.content:encoded: As you are not the original author, you cannot create an official experiment log page for this article.

item.title: Edit Request
item.content:encoded:
I am making this request because i would like to start an official experiment log supplementary page and put the link in the original article. i will be responsible for making the article itself, the format, and i will make a few logs. i will also like to add an addendum in the main page detailing a summary of how effective it is (EX: in it, it says it can replicate SCP-500 but it is not as effective as the original item. i plan to make it semi-unpredictable like SCP-914, where, in general. the more complex an item is, it has a higher chance to be worse in quality. also, if it ends up being a worse quality, depending on the complexity of the item, the duplicate is worse. i know this isnt worded well, so i will provide an example. say that you have a block of steel and a gun. the steel has a lower chance to be a worse quality and a higher chance to be an exact replica, because it is less complex. however, if it were to be worse than the original block, it would only be slightly worse, because it is less complex.
On the other hand, a gun has a high chance to end up worse than the original gun, and since it is more complex, the quality of the item has a tendency to be more degraded when compared to the original than when compared to the difference in quality between the two steel blocks. I will go into more detail in the article and the experiment log will help people to understand. edits to the section i make in order to make it more understandable or correcting errors will be greatly appreciated and i will accept constructive criticism before finalizing the edit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What ended up happening to the 173 clone? Did it eventually time out after a couple weeks, or did it continue indefinitely as a separate anomaly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems to me that only "complex" life forms or anomalous objects are flawed. The anomalous objects definitely makes sense at least.

It gave a human clone a lifespan of two weeks. That's about the lifespan of a fruit, so there may be a correlation there. It is a fruit tree after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noting reversion of non-SPAG changes to article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Attendants… should wear hazmat suits to prevent accidental cloning.

It seems strange that a hazmat suit would prevent cloning when other objects, like candy bars (presumably in wrappers) and toasters (which have internal components) can be cloned just fine.

Can anyone come up with a reason why a hazmat suit would work, or propose a different solution?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't have a strong opinion on the article, but I did enjoy this bit:

Class-D test subject was ordered to break eye contact with clone. When test subject eventually blinked, no movement was observed in cloned material.

"You're kidding me, right? All those precautions when we carried the thing in here- maintain eye contact with the object at all times, bringing twice as many guys as it took to lift it, making us warn each other before we blinked- and now you want me to look away from the copy? On purpose? Screw that!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In addition to the previously mentioned non-clinical bits and LOLFoundation (SCP-173? Really? I understand why they'd want to crosstest nonliving animate material, but there are so many less horrifically dangerous items), this is super redundant. Do we need that many rehashes of the same warning to researchers not to clone Doritos and Pulp Fiction?

This one hasn't aged well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dig around it until they find the roots and haul it in without touching it too much. No harm in the tree cloning ropes/chains. I find it funny to think about it accidentally touching the walls of the facility and cloning a 200 lb model of the base or a 200 lb semitruck that was used to transport it. Use it as transportation around the base. Tiny little diesel trucker.

Honk honk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did they transport it to the facility?…

item.title: Testing, tone...
item.content:encoded:
Surprised no-one's got any problem with the glaring hole: Do the nonliving, non-SCP-500 clones degrade at a similar rate to the living ones, or the 500 clones? If so, how?

Why the heck are the testing log and tone bigger problems than the completely absent angle?

On a side note, I'd also like to see some note further on the tree itself. Even something as small as giving the scientific classification of the tree it appears to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with changing "people" to "humans;" people is non-clinical and has certain connotations that the Foundation would likely want to avoid, especially since, assuming this object clones humans in a similar way to how we understand humans, they would likely still be practically newborns by the time they died of old age. It might be worth mentioning how cloned humans actually function, actually, aside from dying after two weeks.

I don't really have a problem with the notes re: beer, movies-it seems like something that a group of people would do when provided with a resource like this-but the 173 bit is incredibly silly. There's no way that could possibly have turned out well, and certainly no reason to use 173 over something more friendly and less likely to cause a containment breach. That, in my opinion, is far more out-of-character for the Foundation than the bit with the cans of beer.

I was expecting an Yggdrasil analogue when I saw the title, and was pleased when I saw that it was something else entirely, but there's still enough wrong with it in that last section alone to warrant a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: in paragraph 2 of the description, I would recommend changing the word "people" to "Humans" for the sake of professionalism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Been forever; I didn't see the crosstest the first time I read this article.
That said, yeah, the crosstest and/or lolfoundation should seriously be overhauled but it's not exactly in danger of dying as is.
Also the phrase "to prevent accidental cloning" is hilarious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
173? Why not just throw that damned lizard (682) at it? What's the worst that could happen?

Wait, let's try that again, what's the best that could happen? If the answer is "nothing remotely good", probably not a test to undertake. Test it with, idk, coffee beans — is the resulting coffee the same quality as the original beans? Or test if there's a size limit with something light, yarn or thread for example. Or really, anything that isn't a euclid/keter class SCP.

This is me wanting to like it while wanting to run and hide at the thought someone would attempt to clone a keter euclid class SCP.

Editted because apparently 173 is euclid not keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted because it's not good enough to make the cut today. I hear rumors there was a time when this was considered good enough. That was then. There's not enough testing logged (given that you KNOW it's been tested to death) and the test that was approved was nothing short of a deliberate attempt at a catastrophic breech. If that were fixed, I would love to upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find it interesting that no one to this date has attempted to clone safe items with non-physical sapience. Possibility of experimentation with SCP-1230? It would be interesting to see if the intelligence contained within the book was cloned as well as the physical form of the book itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The cloning of SCP-173 turned this into SCP-038-J.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something tells me that SCP-173's underdeveloped clone is really angry with the fact that he was created from SCP-038.
+1
Also, is the replicated 173 still "alive" to this day?

item.title: viable questions
item.content:encoded:
viable questions…..of which I have no answer for.

probably non thou, because it seems like it make's thing's "fruity". you're able to can peaches, cranberry's, etc. etc. without them rotting so I see no reason you couldn't just can said food item's and …. you know not have them rot.

guess I do have a answer!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Replying to old comment only to say: THIS.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's adorable, and using it to clone mundane items is exactly the sort of thing I proudly expect from people. But i have a legitimate question: If the cloned items are of inferior quality and/or last only about two weeks, what implications would that raise with cloned food and digestion? If some preserved or canned food were cloned, would it rot within 2 weeks? If someone ate the cloned food, would it only have a third as much nutritional value, or only provide a third as much relief from hunger? Or would it cause some intestinal issues that might lead to diarrhea?

item.title: Back down to +99
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted.

I am sure this would be downvoted to oblivion if posted today. It lacks imagination, it's not creepy or eerie or unique. It's not particularly funny or entertaining. Cross testing with 173 is retarded. I realize standards were lax in the old days… apparently so were expectations. How this got to +100 is beyond me, but I've done my part to undo it.
Cross testing and LolFoundation. Huh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's one thing for an addenda to portray Foundation personnel as displaying human emotion and weakness, it's another thing entirely if what's implied is incompetence and lack of control to the point of ridiculousness that makes the reader question if such people would really be hired in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my mind, lolFoundation addenda's are not all that bad. They show that even though those working at the foundation try to be very clinical in tone and nature they still have human emotions and weaknesses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens if we put money on it…I would laugh if I put on a hundred, and got back fives. (though it would probably just give back a poor condition hundred)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-173 would never be approved for this, nor would ANY keter or euclid class SCPs. Only the safe, containable ones would, assuming even one SCP would be given approval.

Keep in mind that as of today, this article was created 1720 days ago. Standards weren't as high then as they are now.

I certainly wouldn't be against an extensive experimentation log.

You're welcome to write up said extensive experiment log if you feel the inclination; make sure you save it as a draft in your sandbox page and show it to the IRC chat and/or the drafts forums for feedback prior to a mainsite post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could be amazing, but came off as sub-par. SCP-173 would never be approved for this, nor would ANY keter or euclid class SCPs. Only the safe, containable ones would, assuming even one SCP would be given approval.

Also, with such a heavy implication that clones are of lower quality, I certainly wouldn't be against an extensive experimentation log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of those older ones with a solid concept and a decent rating which would nonetheless benefit from some editing for tone (and possibly the lolfoundationish addenda).

item.title: Re: Log
item.content:encoded: No, we really don't.

item.title: Re: Log
item.content:encoded: No, we really don't.

item.title: Log
item.content:encoded: We need an experiment log of this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it. It's a good safe SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally I find that cross-SCP testing usually results in higher ratings. Or is that experiment logs?

Also, the article isn't very strange. Its like something from D&D.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Though human and animal cloning yields unstable results, using this an an inexhaustible source of skilled and compliant Class-D personnel is a possibility. Especially if mental conditioning is used where the cloned personnel does not believe they're a clone. Basically do what they did in the sci-fi movie Moon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. I'm fairly sure this would get downvoted in a hurry if posted today, especially given the non-metric usage and gratuitous SCP cross-testing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uhm, i don't know if this was noticed by anyone else but… this very same tree is on a walt disney's story about mickey mouse. It is used to clone dollars by an old guy, and by the end of the story donald duck himself is cloned several times, or something like that.

Unfortunately i cannot recall the volume i read this, but i am adamantly sure about having read this story in a walt disney comic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Late post, but I agree. There are plenty of other SCP that meet the criteria stated in the test log. Which wouldn't pose such a risk to D-Class subordinates.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh, DeBeers is already on their way there. Do you know how many diamonds they have stockpiled in an effort to not over-saturate the market? It's like real-estate in that the price has been artificially inflated to the point where it will collapse the moment someone points out that the emperor has no clothes, at least the jewelry market part of it. Now the use of diamonds in electronic equipment, that is much more sustainable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clone diamonds and sell them! Loads of funding for the foundation! De Beers will go bankrupt! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, that's no the most interesting part of the 173 crosstest. It's the fact that the clone moved no matter what happened. Think about a 173 that moves no matter who's looking at it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I never really enjoyed it that much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. It's mostly only cool because it's useful, but then it's the story/article that makes use of it that's cool, not SCP-038 itself. For instance, the most interesting part of this article is the SCP-173 crosstest, but only because of potential insight into SCP-173. There's nothing particularly WRONG with SCP-038, and the article is well-written, but I feel perfectly neutral about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually don't love it. My non-vote is an accurate representation of my opinion on this subject.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doing my part to fix that. *salutes*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm almost certain that nearly everyone who has read this loves this SCP. It's mentioned all the time. WHY is it only +17

item.title: Resources
item.content:encoded: We do need to show that we've at least tried to duplicate Telekill with this thing. Exegetically, it's obvious that it won't work very well, but diegetically we should at least try.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Duplicate 248.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to say… while the first test log for the weight limit is interesting, I can't see ANYONE in their right mind approving an attempt to clone SCP-173

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: so… are cloned items always of poor quality/short lifespan? It's implied by the 30% function of the pills, and the short life of living things, but what if we gave it, say, the Hope Diamond? Would it be flawed, or smaller, or cubic zirconium instead of diamond?
